tests/config/split/unit.tests.tst.description: This folder contains configuration fixtures used by the test suite to validate “split mode” handling of DNS records for a unit.tests.tst zone. The fixtures collectively exercise parsing/rendering of many RR types, multi-value RRsets, subdomain naming, and metadata-driven include/exclude/ignore behaviors. These files matter because they define the expected structured inputs that downstream split/config processors consume during unit tests.
key_behaviors: Represent a split-config unit-test zone as YAML RRset/value fixtures under a common test domain name
Exercise parsing and serialization of multiple DNS RR types with type-specific field shapes (e.g., SRV priority/weight/port, MX preference/exchange, NAPTR service/regex/flags)
Validate multi-value RRsets (lists) where RR type requires multiple targets/values and ordering/preservation matters
Verify relative name handling for records under subdomains (e.g., www vs www.sub; SRV under _srv._tcp)
Confirm metadata controls at the record level (octodns.excluded / octodns.ignored) affect processing outcomes

insights.title: Fixture-driven validation across many RR type schemas
insights.problem: Split-mode logic must correctly interpret heterogeneous RR type schemas and field types (lists, strings, regex-like content) from YAML.
insights.root_cause: Different RR types carry different required/optional structured fields (e.g., SRV/MX/NAPTR/URLFWD), and split-mode pipelines are sensitive to schema mismatches.
insights.solution: Ensure any edits to parsing/validation/rendering preserve the exact YAML-to-internal-field mappings and string escaping expectations embodied by these fixtures.

insights.title: Record-level include/exclude/ignore must be enforced consistently
insights.problem: Records marked as excluded or ignored via octodns metadata must be filtered out during processing.
insights.root_cause: Split-mode pipelines likely evaluate metadata before expanding/merging RRsets, and inconsistent evaluation can cause unexpected records to appear or disappear.
insights.solution: Maintain the precedence and evaluation rules used for octodns.excluded vs octodns.ignored so the exclusion/ignore fixtures continue to align with expected test outputs.

insights.title: Relative and special-subdomain names must map to correct FQDNs
insights.problem: Split-mode record generation must create correct fully qualified names for both standard subdomains and underscore-prefixed service labels.
insights.root_cause: Fixtures include both normal relative names (www/sub) and service-metadata-like labels (_srv._tcp), which can be mishandled by naive name join logic.
insights.solution: Preserve the name resolution/join rules so that generated FQDN record identifiers match what the unit tests expect for each fixture.

insights.title: TXT and regex-like fields require exact string fidelity
insights.problem: TXT values (including semicolons/escaped content) and NAPTR regexp/service strings must survive YAML parsing and DNS rendering without altering semantics.
insights.root_cause: YAML escaping, regex-like characters, and DNS text rendering can interact in ways that change content if transformations are applied.
insights.solution: Keep serialization/parsing transformations strictly content-preserving for these special string fields, including correct escaping behavior.

key_files.why: Core fixture mapping defining the unit.tests.tst zone’s RR types and structured fields used across split-mode tests.
Exercises SRV RRset parsing with multiple targets and type-specific fields (priority/weight/port).
Validates NAPTR complex RRset parsing where regex/flags/order/preference/service fields must be preserved.
Tests filtering behavior using octodns.excluded metadata for a record (CNAME excluded with nested excluded list).
Validates TXT RRset content fidelity including escaped/complex strings relevant to DNS TXT serialization.

construct_relationships: The fixtures in this folder collectively establish the input set for unit.tests.tst split-mode processing, where downstream test code likely loads these YAML files and asserts resulting parsed/merged record sets.
Type-specific fixtures (e.g., SRV, MX, NAPTR, URLFWD) implicitly define the internal data structures that split-mode parsing must populate for each RR type before any merge/split expansion occurs.

Metadata fixtures (excluded.yaml, ignored.yaml, included.yaml) rely on shared record-processing logic that determines whether a record is retained, merged, or filtered out prior to final planning/output.
notable_relationships: These fixtures are downstream dependencies for unit tests that validate split-mode configuration behavior; changes to fixture schema/shape will directly affect those tests’ expectations.

The presence of many RR types suggests tight coupling with a central RR-type parsing/rendering subsystem elsewhere in the project; any modifications to RR schema handling must remain compatible with these fixtures’ YAML shapes and field types.
recommendations: When editing split-mode parsing or record-schema logic, verify compatibility with all RR types represented here (A/AAAA/CNAME/DNAME/MX/NS/PTR/SPF/TXT/SRV/NAPTR/URLFWD), especially their list vs scalar fields and required ordering/serialization behaviors.
Preserve YAML string escaping and content fidelity for TXT and NAPTR regexp-like fields, since tests likely assert exact rendered values or exact parsed field values.

Maintain consistent evaluation and precedence for octodns metadata flags used by these fixtures (octodns.excluded and octodns.ignored), including how they interact with any include/merge behaviors shown by the included.yaml fixture.
Ensure record name resolution remains consistent for both standard subdomain records (e.g., www/sub) and special labels (e.g., _srv._tcp), so that the generated/parsed FQDNs match what tests expect.

tests/config/split/subzone.unit.tests.ts.description: This folder contains YAML fixture data used by unit tests for split configuration behavior at the subzone level. Each file represents a distinct subzone label within the same parent zone (unit.tests.tst), enabling tests to validate that split mode loads the correct per-subzone inputs and renders the expected DNS records. The contents are tightly coupled to test expectations about label naming (numeric vs non-numeric) and the resulting record values.
key_behaviors: Supports split-mode loading where different YAML files map to different subzone labels/record sets
Validates correct handling of both numeric subzone labels (e.g., '2', '12') and non-numeric labels (e.g., 'test')
Ensures rendered A record content matches per-fixture configuration (name/label mapping and IP value)

insights.title: Filename/label mapping affects which subzone is loaded
insights.problem: Changes to fixture naming, structure, or label conventions can break test assumptions about how split mode discovers and associates subzone data.
insights.root_cause: Split tests likely rely on conventions that connect file identity to subzone label and the expected record name.
insights.solution: Preserve the existing mapping semantics (file identity to label, and label to record name) and avoid altering fixture schema that tests may parse deterministically.

insights.title: Numeric vs non-numeric subzone labels are distinct behaviors
insights.problem: Treating label parsing uniformly can cause failures for numeric labels or for labels like 'test' if naming/normalization differs.
insights.root_cause: Test fixtures include both numeric and non-numeric labels to exercise different parsing/handling paths.
insights.solution: When adjusting related parsing or loader logic, verify both numeric and non-numeric label cases still select the correct fixture and produce the correct rendered record.

insights.title: A record values are used as exact assertions
insights.problem: If record generation changes formatting or value handling, assertions tied to specific IPs may fail even if behavior is logically equivalent.
insights.root_cause: Fixtures define concrete A record targets (e.g., 2.4.4.4, 12.4.4.4, 4.4.4.4) that tests likely compare against expected output.
insights.solution: Maintain deterministic rendering/normalization of IP targets and record naming tied to these fixtures.

key_files.why: Fixture for numeric subzone label '2' with a specific A record target; used to validate numeric label handling in split mode.
Fixture for numeric subzone label '12'; exercises multi-digit numeric label parsing/association in split mode.
Fixture for non-numeric subzone label 'test'; validates that split loaders and label normalization support general string labels.

construct_relationships: The fixture files collectively define the expected set of A records for distinct subzone labels within the same parent zone (unit.tests.tst), and the split-mode test harness must select the correct subset based on label/file association.
notable_relationships: These fixtures are consumed by tests elsewhere in the suite that exercise the split configuration loader and/or renderer for subzones under the unit.tests.tst zone; failures here typically indicate cross-folder issues in test discovery, fixture loading, or label-to-record mapping.
recommendations: When editing any loader/parsing/rendering logic that interacts with split configuration, re-validate that subzone label identification still correctly distinguishes numeric ('2','12') from non-numeric ('test') labels and maps them to the right record names.
Maintain fixture schema compatibility (YAML structure and required keys) as tests likely parse these deterministically; ensure changes elsewhere do not alter how these fixtures are interpreted.
Run the associated split/subzone unit test suite to confirm record rendering still produces the exact expected A record targets from these fixtures.

tests/config/dynamic-arpa.description: This folder contains YAML fixtures used by the test suite to exercise dynamic ARPA (reverse DNS) handling. The fixtures focus on tolerance for absent or empty zone definitions and correct recognition of dynamic reverse zones, both for IPv4 in-addr.arpa and IPv6 ip6.arpa. Because these are test configurations rather than production logic, their structure and naming conventions directly influence how test harness code discovers zones and asserts behavior.
key_behaviors: Test data provides intentionally empty or blank YAML zone definitions to validate loader/parsing behavior under minimal input
Fixtures include realistic ARPA zone keys (e.g., in-addr.arpa and long ip6.arpa domains) to ensure zone identification logic triggers correctly even when content is empty
A dedicated unit fixture supplies a small record set to drive PTR/auto-generation or related dynamic ARPA behaviors in isolation

insights.title: Empty YAML must still be considered a valid zone input
insights.problem: Dynamic ARPA processing may incorrectly reject or skip zones when zone definitions are empty/blank.
insights.root_cause: Parsing/loader logic likely distinguishes between missing keys vs. present-but-empty YAML documents; these fixtures ensure correct interpretation.
insights.solution: When editing or reusing these fixtures, preserve their “intentionally empty” characteristics and ensure they remain valid YAML documents that the loader recognizes as zone entries.

insights.title: Zone recognition relies on ARPA domain keying, not record presence
insights.problem: A system might not detect a dynamic ARPA zone if it expects at least one record.
insights.root_cause: Zone discovery/registration may be triggered by the presence of a specific zone name (e.g., ip6.arpa long label) rather than non-empty records.
insights.solution: Maintain the exact ARPA domain labels used as fixture keys so zone identification continues to align with test expectations.

insights.title: Unit fixture includes a “non-zone-file-name” record to validate PTR/dynamic filtering
insights.problem: Dynamic ARPA generation or PTR association may inadvertently treat unrelated records as zone boundaries or zone files.
insights.root_cause: Tests likely verify filtering/selection logic that maps records to PTR outcomes or to whether something should be treated as a zone artifact.
insights.solution: Preserve the presence and distinguishing characteristics of the extra record in the unit fixture so filtering boundaries exercised by tests remain covered.

key_files.why: Primary fixture validating IPv4 reverse dynamic ARPA handling when the YAML zone configuration is intentionally empty.
IPv6 reverse zone fixture with an intentionally blank document to confirm the loader still recognizes the zone from its ARPA key.
Focused fixture containing a small record set plus a deliberately unrelated record to test PTR/dynamic ARPA generation and filtering in isolation.

construct_relationships: The ARPA reverse-zone identification logic in the test harness likely uses the YAML filename/zone key to map the fixture to a specific dynamic ARPA zone
Dynamic ARPA/PTR generation behavior exercised by tests likely consumes record sets from unit.tests.yaml and outputs PTR-related expectations for the corresponding reverse zone(s)
Empty YAML documents likely flow through the same zone-loading path as non-empty ones, with assertions verifying that zone registration/recognition occurs despite missing records
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: test discovery/execution code that reads from tests/config/dynamic-arpa likely expects specific filenames (or derived zone keys) to align with reverse zone naming conventions (in-addr.arpa and ip6.arpa).

Downstream dependency: dynamic ARPA processing and/or PTR-related logic under test will use these fixtures as inputs; changes to fixture structure could affect multiple test cases that rely on these exact fixtures.
recommendations: When modifying fixture YAMLs, ensure they remain valid YAML and preserve the intended emptiness/blankness semantics that the tests rely on.
Preserve the exact ARPA domain labels and filenames because zone discovery/selection in tests may be name-keyed rather than content-keyed.
In unit.tests.yaml, preserve the presence and distinguishing characteristics of the extra non-zone-file-name record used to validate filtering/mapping logic.
Run the relevant test suite that covers dynamic ARPA/PTR behaviors, since changes to fixtures can alter both zone recognition and record-to-PTR association expectations.

tests/config/hybrid.description: This folder contains YAML fixtures used by the test suite to validate “hybrid” DNS zone configuration behavior, specifically around TXT records. The fixtures model both a flat zone file scenario and a split/merged scenario under a subdomain (two.test), including explicit handling for root-label records. These files matter because they encode the expected zone data structures the test harness loads into the system under test.
key_behaviors: Provides static zone data fixtures for tests that exercise hybrid (single vs split source) loading/merging behaviors
Represents TXT record definitions with consistent naming and fixed values to ensure deterministic comparisons
Models special-case semantics for root-label records (empty label '') within a subdomain zone
Supports scenarios where zone content is derived from an inline fixture vs an external split-zone-file fixture

insights.title: Hybrid fixtures encode expected DNS record semantics
insights.problem: Changes to fixture structure or record labeling can break the assumptions made by the test harness when it loads zone data and compares plans/sync outcomes.
insights.root_cause: The fixtures likely map directly onto parsing/normalization logic for zones and records (including root label handling) and are used as canonical inputs.
insights.solution: Maintain exact YAML structure and naming conventions (labels, record types, and values) that the tests rely on; validate that any adjustments remain compatible with the harness loader.

insights.title: Empty label ('') represents zone apex/root behavior
insights.problem: Incorrect representation of the root/apex label can cause missing or mis-scoped records in the merged hybrid zone output.
insights.root_cause: Hybrid configuration tests often treat the apex/root label specially when generating absolute record names and when merging sources.
insights.solution: Preserve the use of the empty-string label ('') for apex/root record fixtures and ensure any edits keep that mapping consistent.

insights.title: Fixture filenames and locations drive test discovery
insights.problem: A misnamed fixture file (including unusual filenames) can prevent it from being discovered/loaded by parameterized tests.
insights.root_cause: Test harnesses frequently glob by filename patterns or derive fixture identity from paths.
insights.solution: Avoid changing fixture filenames or directory placement unless the discovery/loader logic is also updated accordingly; confirm patterns used by the tests.

key_files.why: Canonical flat-zone-file hybrid fixture containing a TXT record used as a baseline input for hybrid-related tests.
Inline YAML fixture dedicated to root-hierarchy TXT record handling under two.test, including the zone root label ('').
Split-zone-file fixture used to validate how hybrid zones are loaded/merged from a split source under two.test.

construct_relationships: The fixture sets under two.test are related as a pair: the root-hierarchy inline YAML fixture (with label '') and the split-zone-file fixture together represent different source pieces that hybrid tests likely merge into a single effective zone view.
The one.test.yaml fixture acts as an independent baseline scenario for hybrid behavior centered on a flat zone file TXT record.
notable_relationships: These fixtures are upstream inputs to the test harness that likely parses YAML into the internal zone/record model; downstream assertions depend on exact record scoping (especially apex/root) and deterministic string values.
recommendations: When editing fixture content, preserve record type (TXT), record names/labels (including any special apex/root empty label usage), and exact string values to keep test expectations stable.
Do not rename or relocate fixture files without confirming the test discovery/loading mechanism that references these paths (including any atypical filename conventions).
Ensure that changes remain compatible with the YAML-to-zone parsing semantics used by the harness, particularly for root/apex record representation and hybrid merge behavior.

tests/config/split.description: This subtree contains YAML fixtures used by the test suite to validate “split mode” configuration handling for DNS zone rendering. It focuses on how per-file inputs are interpreted into records across many RR types, how subzones/labels are mapped to separate fixture files, and how record-level metadata and dynamic routing structures (geo/weighted pools) are represented. These fixtures are tightly coupled to the expected shapes of parsed/serialized configuration structures and therefore constrain how the split-config pipeline behaves.
key_behaviors: Loading split-mode inputs from multiple YAML files and mapping each file to a specific RRset/record name under a shared test zone
Handling label/subzone resolution where numeric and non-numeric labels correspond to different fixture filenames and produce distinct record outputs
Supporting many RR types with type-specific field structures (single values, lists, and multi-entry composite records) in a consistent serialization format
Applying record metadata-driven filtering such as included/excluded/ignored behavior that affects whether records participate in rendering
Processing dynamic routing configurations that include geo-based rule selection and weighted pools for both A/AAAA and CNAME targets, including defaults and multi-rule precedence

insights.title: Type-specific YAML shapes must remain parseable and stable
insights.problem: Each fixture encodes RR type-specific structures (e.g., SRV priority/weight/port, MX preference/exchange ordering, NAPTR flags/order/preference/regexp/service, PTR lists). Any change to schema expectations or serialization can break downstream comparisons in split-mode tests.
insights.root_cause: The fixtures rely on deterministic parsing and output formatting for multiple RRset shapes.
insights.solution: When editing parsing/rendering logic that affects these RR types, preserve the fixture-driven field semantics and ordering/representation assumptions.

insights.title: Subzone fixture filenames define label resolution semantics
insights.problem: Numeric vs non-numeric subzone labels (e.g., '2', '12', 'test') are represented by distinct YAML files and must map to correct record names/labels in output.
insights.root_cause: Split mode likely infers label identity from fixture path/filename and uses that to build record names.
insights.solution: Ensure any changes to file discovery, label normalization, or subzone path parsing keep the mapping consistent with these fixture conventions.

insights.title: octodns.* metadata controls participation in final rendered config
insights.problem: Fixtures marked with octodns.excluded or octodns.ignored are intended to be filtered out, while others exercise included/nested include behavior.
insights.root_cause: The split/config processing pipeline applies record-level metadata rules during merge/split and rendering.
insights.solution: Maintain the precedence and scope of metadata filters (ignored vs excluded vs included nesting) so tests continue to reflect intended inclusion/exclusion behavior.

insights.title: Dynamic geo/weighted pools differ by RR type and must round-trip correctly
insights.problem: Dynamic A/AAAA/CNAME fixtures contain geo rule mappings to pools and weighted values; correctness depends on accurate translation of rules, pool weighting, and defaults into the internal dynamic model and/or rendered output.
insights.root_cause: Dynamic configurations have nested structures and RR-type-specific target/value shapes (IP vs alias/target semantics).
insights.solution: Preserve interpretation of geo codes/rule matching, pool selection precedence across multiple rules, and weighted value representation per RR type.

insights.title: Shared-file fixtures depend on feature flags/conditional inclusion
insights.problem: The shared.yaml fixture is described as intended to be included only when shared-file processing is enabled, meaning tests may conditionally load it based on runtime configuration.
insights.root_cause: The test harness likely toggles inclusion behavior for shared/split config sources.
insights.solution: When modifying split config loading, ensure conditional inclusion behavior remains compatible with the shared fixture expectations.

key_files.why: Represents a simple AAAA RRset fixture used to validate basic split-mode parsing for IPv6 records.
Covers a composite SRV RRset with multiple targets and fields, making it sensitive to ordering and type-specific parsing.
Exercises complex multi-entry NAPTR data including regexp/service fields, requiring fidelity in representation.
Validates dynamic A geo rule selection with weighted IPv4 pools and defaults, covering nested dynamic structures.
Tests split-mode subzone mapping for a numeric label, ensuring correct label/name resolution from filename.

construct_relationships: Dynamic A/AAAA/CNAME fixtures encode nested structures of geo rules mapping to pools of weighted targets; these structures must be interpreted consistently by the same dynamic-routing parser/serializer across RR types.
Unit tests for multiple RR types depend on a shared split-config unit-test zone fixture layout where each YAML file corresponds to specific record names/labels within unit.tests.tst.
Metadata fixtures (ignored/excluded/included) imply a common filtering/merge mechanism that runs during split-mode config processing before record rendering/output comparison.
notable_relationships: These fixtures likely connect to a split-config test harness that performs file discovery and loads YAML into the octodns configuration model, so changes in loader/path resolution/normalization will affect many fixtures at once.

Dynamic routing fixtures probably depend on the same internal dynamic model used by planners/providers for generating final DNS records; failures in model translation will manifest in multiple dynamic.* fixture tests.
recommendations: Preserve YAML schema compatibility for all covered RR types (including list vs scalar fields and per-entry field ordering) since fixtures encode deterministic expected representations.
Treat filename-derived label/subzone mapping as a behavioral contract; any change to normalization or mapping logic must remain consistent with both numeric and non-numeric fixture filenames.
Maintain exact semantics and precedence of record metadata handling (octodns.excluded, octodns.ignored, and included/nested inclusion blocks) as tests depend on filtering outcomes.

Ensure dynamic geo/weighted pool interpretation (rule matching, default selection, weight handling, and RR-type-specific value/target shapes) remains consistent with fixture structure across A, AAAA, and CNAME.

octodns/record.description: The `octodns/record` package defines octoDNS’ core DNS record domain model: a registry-driven `Record` base with shared validation/normalization, plus per-RR-type value and record implementations. It matters because plan/sync logic depends on consistent parsing of provider “RDATA text” into structured value objects, deterministic serialization for diffs/checksums, and uniform handling of special cases like IDNA normalization and templated values. The folder also exposes a stable public API surface by re-exporting all record/value classes for use by higher-level parsing, planning, and rendering layers.
key_behaviors: Builds concrete record instances via a global registry based on record type, enforcing invariant validation and name normalization before type-specific checks
Parses provider-specific “RDATA text” into structured dict-like value objects with per-field validation and canonical rendering back to RFC-style text
Supports templated values (presence of `{...}`) by selectively bypassing validation/normalization and formatting value fields during rendering
Ensures deterministic serialization for plan/sync consumers by producing stable `_data()`/`data` representations and equality tuples across value objects
Generates plan changes (Create/Update/Delete and special dynamic/geo-driven Update behavior) when record payloads differ

insights.title: Record data omitted `octodns` special section
insights.problem: The `Record.data` representation did not include the `_octodns` special section, which can cause copy/serialization and downstream diffs/checksums to diverge from the in-memory model.
insights.root_cause: `Record.data`/`Record._data()` behavior did not carry forward the `Record._octodns` metadata into the exported dict.
insights.solution: Any edits affecting record serialization must preserve inclusion/exclusion semantics for `_octodns` special data consistently across `data()`, `copy()`, and checksum consumers.

insights.title: Empty-string values mishandled due to truthiness
insights.problem: Copying records with empty values (e.g., `value=''`) did not behave correctly because logic relied on truthiness checks rather than explicit `None` handling.
insights.root_cause: Use of `if value:` / `elif values:` patterns rather than distinguishing empty strings from absent values.
insights.solution: When modifying value/copy logic, ensure explicit `None` vs empty-string/list semantics are preserved so diffs and rendered output remain deterministic.

insights.title: Healthcheck field semantics assumed HTTP(S)
insights.problem: Healthcheck properties applied incorrectly to non-HTTP(S) protocols and healthcheck protocol validation was incomplete, potentially producing invalid record payloads or diffs.
insights.root_cause: Validation/rendering logic assumed HTTP/HTTPS-style host/path semantics regardless of the healthcheck protocol.
insights.solution: Changes to base validation or healthcheck processing must keep protocol-dependent inclusion/validation rules aligned with the record types that actually support those fields.

insights.title: TXT/SPF chunk boundary escaping safety
insights.problem: Chunking must not split in a way that leaves an unfinished escape sequence (e.g., trailing backslash), otherwise output becomes syntactically invalid or provider-rejected.
insights.root_cause: Chunking works on escaped text and must account for boundary behavior of escape sequences.
insights.solution: When adjusting chunk size/escaping or parsing, preserve the boundary checks and delimiter joining semantics used to render multi-string RDATA.

insights.title: Templating support varies by record/value type
insights.problem: Some value types treat templating placeholders differently (supported vs bypassed), leading to inconsistent validation bypass behavior and potential persistent diffs.
insights.root_cause: Per-value `template()` implementations are not fully uniform across types, and not all record values support placeholders for all fields.
insights.solution: Edits must respect each value type’s templating contract—especially which fields are formatted vs which are validated/bypassed when `{...}` is present.

insights.title: Target FQDN validation and null targets are centralized but protocol-specific
insights.problem: Incorrect propagation of record-type/key semantics into `validate_target_fqdn()` can yield inconsistent validation errors or allow/disallow incorrect null targets.
insights.root_cause: Shared target validation centralizes common logic (IDNA, trailing dot, templating bypass, `.` null handling) but depends on `_type` and key naming to behave correctly.
insights.solution: When changing callers or field keying for target-like values (MX exchange, SRV target, SVCB targetname, Alias/CNAME/DNAME), preserve the exact key and record-type behavior expected by the shared validator.

insights.title: Change JSON missing per-change `name` field
insights.problem: Serialized plan change entries lacked top-level `name`, making it harder to consistently attribute changes to record names in consumers.
insights.root_cause: Change serialization did not include expected top-level fields for downstream reporting/checksums.
insights.solution: When editing `Change`/`Create`/`Update`/`Delete` serialization, ensure the exported schema remains consistent with any checksum computation and plan/sync consumers.

key_files.why: Central `Record` base class with registry-driven construction, invariant validation, IDNA/name normalization, and core serialization/copy pathways that all record types rely on.
Shared hostname/target validation and wrapper value types used by multiple RR types (MX/SRV/SVCB/Alias/CNAME/DNAME-like targets) to enforce consistent IDNA and templating/null rules.
Defines the package public API via re-exports, which higher-level planning/sync/parsing code likely imports from `octodns.record` directly.
Models plan changes (Create/Update/Delete) with serialized payload expectations used by sync planning/reporting and potentially checksums.
Implements dynamic routing/geo-like payload validation and emits update changes when dynamic content differs, affecting how plan changes are generated.

construct_relationships: Concrete record classes (e.g., `ARecord`, `AaaaRecord`, `MxRecord`, `SrvRecord`, `NaptrRecord`, `CaaRecord`, `CnameRecord`, `AliasRecord`, `DnameRecord`, `DsRecord`, `SvcbRecord`) register themselves with the shared `Record` registry via `Record.register_type(...)` and rely on `Record.new()` to instantiate and validate inputs.

`Record.new()`/`Record.validate()` perform shared invariants (name normalization via IDNA, ttl/name lengths, healthcheck protocol rules) before invoking record/value-specific `parse_rdata_text`, `validate`, `process`, and `template` methods on the appropriate value objects.

Target-like value objects (`_TargetValue` specializations such as `AliasValue`, `CnameValue`, `DnameValue`, plus MX exchange/SRV target/SVCB targetname) delegate hostname correctness to `validate_target_fqdn()` in `octodns/record/target.py`, with behavior determined by `_type` and the field key passed by each record/value type.

`Change` (Create/Update/Delete) uses record object references and serializable data derived from record `_data()`/`Record.data` outputs; record serialization consistency therefore directly impacts plan/change payloads.

Specialized value renderers (e.g., `MxValue`, `SrvValue`, `NaptrValue`, `SvcbValue`, `Chunked` helpers, `CaaValue`) implement `template()`/rendering that must align with base record’s templating bypass and normalization semantics.

notable_relationships.relationship: Plan/Sync layers rely on stable `Record.data`/serialization and `Change` payloads defined here
notable_relationships.evidence_from_folder: Change serialization and record data representations are used for plan output/checksums; base serialization governs equality/diffing.

notable_relationships.relationship: `octodns/record` value parsing/rendering underpins higher-level provider/source integration
notable_relationships.evidence_from_folder: Multiple record types parse provider RDATA text into structured value dicts; those structured objects are what other layers compare to desired config and render for providers.

notable_relationships.relationship: Dynamic/geo routing support in record values affects update generation
notable_relationships.evidence_from_folder: Dynamic mixins validate/serialize and emit changes when dynamic payload differs; this couples record-level diffing with sync planning decisions.

recommendations: Preserve the exact registry/construction expectations of `Record.register_type` and the selection logic in `Record.new()`; record-type naming and uniqueness constraints impact instantiation across the whole system.

When altering serialization/copy/equality paths (`Record._data`, `Record.data`, value equality tuples), maintain deterministic output and ensure special `_octodns` metadata continues to be handled exactly as existing consumers expect.

Treat templating as a per-value-type contract: validate/bypass behavior and which fields are formatted must remain consistent with current implementations to avoid persistent diffs or invalid provider output.

Keep shared target validation semantics intact (`validate_target_fqdn` rules for IDNA normalization, trailing dots, null targets, and templating bypass) and ensure any callers pass the correct `_type` and `key` identifiers.

Maintain record/value parsing/rendering correctness for escape/chunking (TXT/SPF via chunked helpers) and for tokenization edge cases (e.g., CAA/LEX-like parsing constraints), since these affect provider RDATA compatibility and diffing stability.
When modifying `Change` and related serialization schema, ensure plan/sync consumers and checksum logic continue to receive the expected fields and stable representations.

octodns/processor.description: The octodns/processor folder contains the pluggable processing components that run during octoDNS’s zone-to-plan and plan/sync lifecycle. These processors mutate desired/target zone record sets, validate constraints (TTL/SPF), generate derived records (e.g., ARPA PTR, ACME ownership handling), and filter computed plan changes based on ownership/metadata conventions. Because processors can both transform and selectively suppress plan changes, correctness depends on shared assumptions about record mutability, lenient behavior, and consistent handling of record value types (including trailing-dot normalization and templating).
key_behaviors: Executes in multiple pipeline phases (at least source-zone, target-zone, source+target, and plan-processing hooks) with no-op defaults provided by a shared base class
Mutates Zone record collections in-place by replacing records or removing/adding records, using record copy/replace semantics expected by the rest of octoDNS
Enforces configuration-driven policies by validating records and raising processor-specific exceptions that block planning/sync when violations occur
Generates synthetic/derived DNS records (PTR for reverse mappings; ownership TXT; meta TXT; ACME challenge handling) that influence plan computation and change filtering

insights.title: Lenient behavior must be preserved consistently across processors
insights.problem: Multiple processors either skip validation when records are lenient or propagate lenient settings when recreating records. Some processors referenced internal/private record state (e.g., _octodns) or did not pass lenient through templated/copied records, risking incorrect strictness or unexpected failures.
insights.root_cause: Inconsistent access to lenient state and inconsistent use of lenient propagation when processors create/replace records.
insights.solution: When editing or integrating processors, ensure lenient checks use the stable, intended interface and that any processor creating new records (templating/trailing dots/meta/ownership) passes the correct lenient context so validation and plan strictness remain consistent.

insights.title: Processor mutations must preserve RR value concrete classes
insights.problem: Trailing-dot normalization previously coerced RR value types back to plain strings, breaking downstream code that expects specialized value objects (e.g., ALIAS/CNAME/DNAME representations with methods).
insights.root_cause: Value-field normalization that reconstructs values without preserving the original value class/structure.
insights.solution: Edits must respect that some processors rely on value objects retaining their concrete types; ensure any transformations preserve or re-wrap using the original value types/classes as expected by other processors/providers.

insights.title: Processors selectively suppress or filter plan changes based on sentinel/identity conventions
insights.problem: MetaProcessor suppresses specific single-change plans; OwnershipProcessor filters plan changes to only records considered owned (plus specific exceptions like meta). ACME managing logic tags and treats ACME TXT records specially, removing unrelated ACME-like TXT records.
insights.root_cause: Processors implement identity heuristics (sentinel tags, record ownership markers, meta record keys) that directly shape which changes survive into the plan and sync stages.
insights.solution: When modifying these processors or plan-change handling, maintain the existing conventions for identifying owned/meta/sentinel/tagged records; otherwise plan contents can change unexpectedly.

insights.category: Robustness: null/empty value handling in filters
insights.title: Value-based filter processors must handle None/empty record values safely
insights.problem: Value allow/reject filters can crash when record.value is None because they assume the presence of a value and attempt to access .rdata_text on it.
insights.root_cause: Missing defensive handling for records whose value field is None/empty for the targeted record types.
insights.solution: Ensure any edits to filter/match logic handle None-valued records according to the pipeline’s expectations (either treat as non-match, skip, or explicitly validate), without throwing attribute errors.

insights.category: Performance/cost: DNS lookup recursion in SPF validation
insights.title: SPF DNS lookup counting and include: recursion can amplify work
insights.problem: SPF validation counts DNS lookup cost per mechanism and recursively resolves include: domains via DNS TXT queries, potentially producing substantial network work and complexity.
insights.root_cause: Recursive DNS resolution without clear caching/limits in the processor behavior.
insights.solution: Any changes should respect existing counting semantics (RFC-aligned 10 lookup cap logic and recursive include effects) and avoid altering cost-calculation meaning.

insights.title: TTL constraint processors must validate configuration relationships
insights.problem: TtlClampProcessor clamps to min/max; restriction processors enforce allowed sets/ranges. Misconfiguration (e.g., min_ttl > max_ttl) can create inverted behavior or incorrect blocking.
insights.root_cause: Insufficient validation or reliance on implicit behavior of inverted ranges.
insights.solution: Edits should preserve or strengthen configuration validation and exception types/messages so pipeline callers can reliably interpret failures.

insights.category: Zone-specific derived generation: ARPA and wildcard behavior
insights.title: Auto ARPA generation depends on zone suffix matching and wildcard handling
insights.problem: AutoArpa generates PTR candidates only when ARPA keys match the reverse zone name suffix, and it historically ignored wildcard A/AAAA records by default (with an optional wildcard_replacement).
insights.root_cause: Eligibility rules combine record-name patterns, IP expansion, wildcard replacement, and ARPA zone suffix constraints.
insights.solution: Maintain these eligibility/suffix/wildcard conventions so reverse zones get consistent PTR records without leaking into unrelated reverse namespaces.

key_files.why: Defines the processor API, lifecycle hook methods, and shared exception/lenient semantics that all other processors depend on.
Implements ownership TXT injection and plan change filtering, which directly controls what sync/apply will affect.
Creates/updates the special meta TXT record and can suppress specific plan changes, impacting plan determinism.
Handles ACME DNS-01 TXT challenge management via tagging/sentinel logic and cleanup of ACME-like records.
Generates reverse PTR records from desired A/AAAA inputs; correctness depends on ARPA name computation and eligibility constraints.

construct_relationships: All processors in this folder extend BaseProcessor and implement one or more lifecycle hooks (e.g., process_source_zone, process_source_and_target_zones, process_plan) following the base class’s no-op defaults contract

Templating processor modifies desired zone records by rendering values via value.template(params) when present, then replaces records in the desired zone so downstream processors/plan computation see rendered values
EnsureTrailingDots mutates desired zone records by replacing specific RR fields/values with trailing-dot-normalized equivalents while preserving the original value types expected by later logic

OwnershipProcessor adds synthetic ownership TXT records during source-zone processing and later filters plan changes in process_plan based on ownership identification across desired and existing records

MetaProcessor adds/updates a special meta TXT record during source+target processing and then may suppress plan changes if the only change is the meta record update deemed up-to-date by key-set matching
Filter processors remove records from zone.records in-place (source vs target semantics governed by include_target) based on matchers for type/name/value/network/NS rules
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: processors assume Zone/Record APIs that support record iteration, copying, lenient flags, and replace semantics when adding/updating records (used across ownership/meta/acme/templating/trailing_dots).
Plan/sync dependency: processors that modify plan changes (OwnershipProcessor, MetaProcessor) rely on the structure of the computed plan/change objects produced elsewhere in octoDNS.
External dependency: SPF processor performs DNS TXT lookups (including recursive include: resolution), coupling processor behavior to network/DNS resolver semantics.
recommendations: Preserve processor lifecycle hook signatures and phase behavior expectations (source-zone vs target-zone vs plan) to avoid changing which transformations/filters apply when.

When editing processors that create or replace records (templating, trailing dots, meta, ownership, acme), ensure lenient mode and record identity semantics are carried forward so downstream validations and plan comparisons remain consistent.
Maintain correctness of record value type handling for RR-specific structured values; avoid coercing specialized value objects into plain strings when normalizing fields.
Do not change the identity conventions used for sentinel/meta/ownership/ACME tagging unless you also adjust corresponding plan filtering/suppression logic and any consumers’ assumptions.
For SPF and ARPA processors, keep eligibility rules and counting/name-derivation semantics intact, since these determine which derived records or exceptions appear in plans.

octodns/provider.description: The octodns/provider subtree defines the provider abstraction and the planning primitives that sit between configured providers (as both sources and targets) and the sync plan output. It matters because nearly all sync behavior flows through BaseProvider’s populate/desired/existing processing pipeline and through Plan’s safety checks and rendering. Changes here have cross-cutting impact on processors, record normalization, lenient/strict behavior, and the shape of plan output emitted to CLI and other consumers.
key_behaviors: Provider planning lifecycle: build an existing Zone from the provider backend, copy desired state, normalize both sides, then compute record diffs into a change list
Provider-specific normalization hooks for desired and existing zones that can add/remove/transform records before diffing
Two-phase processor pipeline that first prepares the target zone, then processes cross-source/target interactions before changes are computed
Plan computation that enforces deterministic output ordering and applies safety thresholds (including precedence rules) before allowing risky operations
Plan rendering to multiple output formats (logger/json/markdown/html) including meta payload propagation
Provider-to-file I/O for YAML zones when used as a target, including discovery of zone inputs and record serialization semantics
Capability signalling for supported record behaviors (via SUPPORTS_* flags and registered record types) to allow capability-aware planning

insights.title: Lenient mode threading across existing-zone processing and processor hooks
insights.problem: Lenient handling was not consistently propagated into the provider’s existing-zone processing flow, and processor hooks may not accept a lenient kwarg, risking runtime failures or inconsistent behavior.
insights.root_cause: Multiple call sites/overrides: BaseProvider’s _process_existing_zone path and processor hook invocation paths do not share a single consistent lenient contract across provider versions.
insights.solution: Ensure lenient behavior is applied uniformly across both desired and existing normalization and that any lenient kwarg to processors/providers is guarded for backwards compatibility when hook signatures differ.

insights.title: Root NS record warning/diagnostic toggling
insights.problem: Root NS support diagnostics can require a user-controlled toggle, since providers may warn (and potentially remove records) when root NS records are missing or unsupported.
insights.root_cause: Plan and/or provider logic introduces Root NS related behavior as a cross-cutting concern, but users needed an explicit way to disable such diagnostics/warnings.
insights.solution: Treat root NS warnings as a configurable behavior controlled via a dedicated toggle (e.g., root_ns_warnings) and ensure it is consistently applied in both planning and any provider normalization that could remove or alter root NS records.

insights.title: Plan safety thresholds and Root NS change detection semantics
insights.problem: Apply safety depends on computed counts/percentages of change types, with precedence rules across provided defaults and zone-specific settings; additionally, plans raise when any change targets the zone root NS record.
insights.root_cause: Safety evaluation is centralized in Plan and relies on specific change-class naming and deterministic sorting of changes prior to counting/validation.
insights.solution: When editing plan logic, preserve change-class mapping and ordering assumptions, and maintain consistent Root NS change detection criteria (name '' / root NS target) so validations remain predictable.

insights.title: Plan output file handle parameterization
insights.problem: Plan output classes require support for per-output files while still allowing default behavior to write to a provided file handle; earlier refactors required parameter naming/routing changes.
insights.root_cause: Plan output plumbing likely supports both file handles and output filename variants, with different renderer implementations needing a consistent contract.
insights.solution: Preserve the output parameter semantics and routing mechanism expected by all Plan renderer implementations so changes don’t break CLI/automation consumers.

insights.title: Plan meta exposure limitations for extensibility
insights.problem: Plan output did not expose structured data for non-record operations, limiting provider extensibility and observability beyond record diffs.
insights.root_cause: Plan’s data model appears focused on record changes and renders meta but not a structured payload for other operation types.
insights.solution: When extending Plan or provider change models, ensure meta and any additional operation descriptors are represented in a way that all renderers can consume without losing information.

insights.category: YAML Provider Input Discovery & Multi-Source Robustness
insights.title: ignore_missing_zones requirement for multi-source setups
insights.problem: YamlProvider raised an exception when no zone YAML inputs were found for a zone, which is undesirable in multi-source configurations where some directories may not contain the zone.
insights.root_cause: populate() discovery logic treats absence of inputs as an error rather than a configurable behavior.
insights.solution: Ensure ignore_missing_zones semantics are honored across discovery and populate pathways, especially when a provider is optional as a source.

insights.title: TXT/SPF escaped semicolon unescaping and key reference bug
insights.problem: When escaped_semicolons=False, TXT/SPF values still contained escaped semicolons (\;), leading to incorrect record content; an earlier bug referenced the wrong key (d[''] instead of d['value']).
insights.root_cause: Serialization/deserialization logic for TXT/SPF escaping/unescaping likely uses incorrect mapping keys and inconsistent escaping rules across modes.
insights.solution: Correctly unescape semicolons for TXT/SPF when escaped_semicolons is disabled and ensure the correct fields are used when transforming record data.

key_files.why: Defines BaseProvider, the core provider lifecycle and the hooks that normalize desired/existing zones and invoke processors before diffing.
Implements Plan safety validations, change counting/threshold precedence, Root NS detection, and all plan rendering output formats.
Implements YamlProvider as both a source and optional target, including zone discovery, populate semantics, and TXT/SPF escaping rules.
Defines provider exception types used by provider implementations to signal generic failures vs capability/support issues.

construct_relationships: BaseProvider.plan orchestrates populate() for existing Zone, copies desired Zone, invokes _process_desired_zone/_process_existing_zone normalization, runs processor hooks, then computes diffs via existing.changes(desired, provider) to produce a changes list for Plan
BaseProvider invokes processor pipeline stages (process_target_zone and process_source_and_target_zones) before final diff computation; these stages affect the final changes list consumed by Plan

Plan sorts the passed changes list for deterministic output and then derives safety checks from change-class types/counts, including RootNsChange detection for changes targeting the root NS record (name '' / zone root)

YamlProvider implements populate() and (when acting as target) _apply(plan) in a way consistent with the BaseProvider plan/change model, including record creation semantics and TXT/SPF value escaping/unescaping
notable_relationships: Upstream: Providers are used by the manager/sync orchestration layer to produce existing/desired zone diffs and plan objects; any BaseProvider or Plan signature/behavior changes affect the global sync workflow.
Downstream: Plan renderers likely feed CLI outputs (logger/json/markdown/html) and any consumer expectations about file/handle semantics and meta payload shape must remain consistent.

Cross-cutting: Processor hooks are invoked from BaseProvider and may have signature variations; Plan and provider normalization behaviors must remain compatible with processor and record model expectations.
recommendations: Preserve BaseProvider’s contract for how desired/existing Zones are copied, normalized, and diffed into changes, including the ordering/inputs to processors and the semantics of provider hooks.

When adjusting lenient behavior or validation flows, ensure lenient mode is applied consistently across existing-zone processing and processor/provider hook invocation while maintaining backwards compatibility for hook signatures.
Maintain Plan’s deterministic change ordering and the mapping between change classes and safety threshold counting logic, as well as Root NS change detection criteria.
Keep Plan output rendering contracts stable (output destination handling, meta payload rendering) so CLI/automation consumers do not break due to output plumbing changes.
For YamlProvider, respect ignore_missing_zones behavior in populate/discovery paths and ensure TXT/SPF escaping/unescaping semantics remain correct across both source and target modes.

tests/config.description: tests/config is a collection of YAML fixtures that drive integration-style validation of octoDNS configuration parsing, dynamic behaviors, and safety checks. These files encode edge cases (invalid class/module references, malformed plan outputs, alias cycles, optional None/null handling) that constrain how the manager, loaders, and planning/sync pipelines must interpret configuration. Because these fixtures are consumed by test harness discovery and assertions, their exact structure and naming directly affect what code paths are exercised.
key_behaviors: Validation fails fast on malformed manager.plan_outputs and provider class references (missing fields, missing module qualifiers, non-importable classes).
Zone resolution must correctly detect alias chains and prevent infinite recursion when alias cycles are present or can be made cyclic by configuration.
Dry-run semantics propagate per-zone and respect explicit overrides on subzones, even when multiple input/output YAML providers are configured.
Processor configuration is interpreted as a chain: known processors run and unknown processors trigger error handling without producing unintended side effects.
Dynamic ARPA generation tolerates empty/absent zone configuration inputs while still recognizing dynamic reverse zones and applying auto-generation rules consistently.
Dynamic configuration modes require provider capabilities such as list_zones; when unsupported, the dynamic workflow must fail or degrade according to defined expectations.
Hybrid zone fixtures model deterministic TXT record structures across flat vs split/merged source scenarios, including root-label ('') handling.
Dynamic routing fixtures expand complex routing policies (pools, geo, weighted, fallback) into provider-specific record outputs in a stable, testable way.

insights.title: Plan output configuration schema enforcement
insights.problem: Malformed manager.plan_outputs fixtures intentionally include invalid/missing fields to ensure the CLI/manager rejects bad configuration early.
insights.root_cause: Missing required schema attributes or incorrect types for plan output class definitions.
insights.solution: Tests depend on strict validation behavior and predictable error surfaces; changes to schema parsing must preserve failure modes and error timing.

insights.title: Tolerance of empty/blank dynamic reverse zone configs
insights.problem: Dynamic ARPA fixtures include intentionally empty YAML zone definitions while still expecting zone recognition and processing.
insights.root_cause: Loader logic may assume zone content exists and fail when zone dicts are empty or null-like.
insights.solution: Maintain compatibility with empty-zone representations so dynamic workflows can proceed without crashing and still identify the zone keys.

insights.title: Alias cycle detection in zone resolution
insights.problem: Alias chains can be cyclic via configuration; without detection, alias resolution risks infinite recursion.
insights.root_cause: Zone alias traversal lacks cycle detection or uses insufficient visited-state tracking.
insights.solution: Cycle detection and error/handling semantics are test-asserted; changes to resolution logic must retain deterministic cycle handling.

insights.title: Per-zone always-dry-run overrides
insights.problem: Dry-run behavior must respect precedence where one zone forces always-dry-run while a subzone explicitly disables it.
insights.root_cause: Precedence/propagation rules for inherited zone settings may be ambiguous or order-dependent.
insights.solution: Preserve the exact precedence/override semantics across planning/apply workflows; tests encode expected inherited vs overridden outcomes.

insights.title: Dynamic config requires zone discovery support
insights.problem: A dynamic-config fixture uses a provider that does not support list_zones while defining zones that depend on discovery.
insights.root_cause: Dynamic manager mode may not check provider capabilities before relying on list_zones.
insights.solution: Keep capability checks and resulting behavior consistent so tests can verify correct failure/degradation when zone listing is unavailable.

insights.title: Optional None values in YAML should not crash initialization
insights.problem: Fixtures include empty sections and explicit null values for optional keys to ensure initialization is resilient.
insights.root_cause: Config-to-object mapping may mishandle YAML nulls (e.g., expecting dict/list but receiving None).
insights.solution: Ensure config parsing preserves stable defaults and avoids attribute errors; maintain behavior with explicit YAML nulls.

insights.title: Root-label TXT record representation in hybrid configs
insights.problem: Hybrid fixtures encode TXT records under both flat and split/merged scenarios, including the special root label ('').
insights.root_cause: Serialization/deserialization may treat empty labels or root label differently across formats.
insights.solution: Keep root-label handling consistent so deterministic comparisons in tests remain valid.

key_files.why: Primary fixture for exercising alias resolution cycle detection and preventing infinite recursion.
Validates dry-run inheritance and explicit subzone override precedence across planning/apply flows.
Triggers configuration validation for manager.plan_outputs with invalid schema fields in a plan output class mapping.
Covers dynamic auto-ARPA population behavior when normal sources are absent, including TTL/replace semantics.
Models hybrid configuration for a subdomain with a root-label TXT record, constraining root-label parsing/serialization.

construct_relationships: Manager-level config loading (from YAML providers and manager.* fields) must feed into zone resolution logic that handles aliases (including cycle detection) before planning/sync can proceed.
Plan output configuration (manager.plan_outputs) is validated during startup/initialization and must block execution when malformed, independent of provider/zone correctness.
Provider class resolution logic consumes fully-qualified class names and module qualifiers from config; failures must surface during configuration validation rather than at runtime.
Dry-run behavior is computed from per-zone flags (always-dry-run) and propagated into downstream plan/apply decision paths with subzone override precedence.
Processor configuration is interpreted as part of a pipeline applied to records; unknown processor names must be treated as configuration errors that affect the ability to render a plan.
notable_relationships: test harnesses likely discover fixtures by path under tests/config and may rely on file naming conventions and directory structure (e.g., tests/config/dynamic-arpa, tests/config/hybrid) to assemble inputs.

Fixtures describing YAML providers and processors assume alignment with real provider/processor loading mechanisms used in octoDNS; altering configuration schema or loader behavior may break multiple fixtures simultaneously.
Dynamic ARPA and dynamic routing fixtures imply deeper integrations between manager auto_arpa/dynamic expansion logic and the serialization format expected by YAML dump providers used in tests.
recommendations: Preserve the structure and key conventions of the YAML fixtures (including empty/blank zone configs and root-label '') because test expectations are sensitive to parsed shapes, not just content.

When changing config parsing/validation logic, maintain the same failure timing and error surfacing for malformed plan_outputs and provider class references, as these fixtures assert early rejection behavior.

Ensure any modifications to alias resolution, dry-run precedence, processor pipeline handling, and dynamic config capability checks remain consistent with the edge-case semantics encoded by these fixtures.

Run the full set of tests that consume these fixtures (especially those covering dynamic ARPA, dynamic routing, hybrid TXT, and alias loop handling) because changes in shared parsing/loader components can cascade.

octodns/cmds.description: The octodns/cmds folder contains the command-layer entry points for the octoDNS CLI. These modules translate user-provided CLI options into calls against the core orchestration layer (Manager), then format outputs and select process exit codes based on results. Because they handle logging setup, concurrency for live DNS queries, and orchestration flags (dry-run vs apply), changes here can affect operator-visible behavior and the determinism of CLI outputs.
key_behaviors: Standardizes CLI argument parsing and immediately configures Python logging (including log routing to stdout/stderr and optional syslog or YAML logging config).
Loads configured DNS zones and data sources via a central Manager, then performs operations (compare, dump, validate, report, sync) with operator-controlled flags and exit codes.
For live DNS reporting, executes concurrent resolver queries with bounded concurrency and normalizes failures into stable sentinel outputs for consistent reporting/aggregation.
For sync operations, orchestrates a plan/apply workflow with default dry-run semantics, optional force/force-like behavior, and optional checksum gating.

insights.title: Log-level formatting compatibility impacts operator readability
insights.problem: WARNING output formatting was misaligned due to deprecated/renamed log levels (e.g., WARN vs WARNING), producing inconsistent-looking log lines.
insights.root_cause: The logging configuration/formatting logic likely mapped CLI flags to different level names than those expected by the configured formatter/handlers.
insights.solution: Ensure log level naming and formatter expectations remain consistent across CLI logging setup and any configured YAML logging settings.

insights.title: TXT record value quoting/escaping can be unintentionally double-escaped
insights.problem: TXT record strings were unnecessarily escaped (double-quoted) because upstream query/serialization produced quotes that JSON handling re-escaped.
insights.root_cause: TXT values likely contained surrounding quotes that were preserved through the DNS query output path and then treated again as string content during JSON serialization.
insights.solution: Validate end-to-end TXT normalization so that surrounding quotes are stripped or consistently represented before JSON encoding.

insights.title: Async resolver query output ordering affects consistency checks
insights.problem: Deterministic sorting removed/changed in the async report path; consistency logic may depend on answer ordering or the chosen representation key.
insights.root_cause: Asynchronous query execution and subsequent aggregation can change answer ordering unless answers are explicitly sorted before both display and consistency computation.
insights.solution: Keep answer ordering and the consistency keying strategy aligned so that 'same answers' detection is stable regardless of resolver response order.

insights.title: Sync apply must correspond to computed plan semantics
insights.problem: Operators require a mechanism to ensure apply proceeds with the exact plan computed during the dry-run phase; otherwise drift could cause unintended changes.
insights.root_cause: Plan computation and apply execution can be separated in time/conditions; without gating (e.g., checksum), apply may not reflect the earlier plan inputs.
insights.solution: Treat checksum gating and force/doit semantics as the contract ensuring apply corresponds to the prior computed plan.

key_files.why: Defines shared CLI argument parsing and central logging configuration behavior used by other command entry points.
Implements the live DNS reporting command using asyncio concurrency, resolver input normalization, aggregation, and output formatting.
Orchestrates the plan/apply flow for syncing changes, including default dry-run behavior, force semantics, and checksum gating.
Computes and prints differences between two configured sources for a zone, including ignore-prefix filtering and exit codes.
Implements validation that turns logged warnings into process failure (exit code 1) via a custom logging handler.

construct_relationships: All command modules depend on shared CLI argument/logging utilities from octodns/cmds/args.py to ensure consistent CLI behavior and logging setup.
compare/dump/report/validate/sync instantiate and use the core Manager to load zones from configured sources and then perform their respective operations (compare/dump/report/validate/sync).
validate uses a custom logging handler (FlaggingHandler) to intercept Manager/record validation warnings and convert them into a non-zero exit status.
report constructs async tasks for record/resolver combinations and aggregates DNS answers into consistency computations used for output generation.
sync performs a two-phase workflow (compute plan, then optionally apply) and gates apply behavior based on CLI flags (dry-run default, force/doit, optional checksum match).
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: These CLI commands depend on the core orchestration layer (Manager) and its contract for loading configs, validating records, dumping zone data, and producing sync plans.

Downstream dependency: Output formats (CSV/JSON for report; printed diffs for compare; filesystem output for dump; exit codes for validate/sync) are consumed by operators/scripts and therefore are compatibility-sensitive.
Cross-cutting concern: Logging configuration and handler behaviors in args.py and validate.py directly influence whether warnings are surfaced and whether processes exit with failure.
recommendations: When editing any command module, preserve the CLI contract: argument meanings, default behaviors (especially sync dry-run), and exit code semantics expected by automation.
Maintain logging-level naming and formatting consistency across CLI flags and any YAML-based logging configuration to avoid operator-visible log regressions.
For report output, ensure DNS answer normalization (including TXT quoting/escaping) and any consistency computation remain stable and deterministic under concurrency.
For sync, preserve the integrity mechanism (checksum gating and how/when it is checked) so that apply behavior cannot diverge from the computed plan.

octodns/secret.description: The octodns/secret folder defines the abstraction and concrete implementation for resolving sensitive configuration values from external secret sources. It currently centers on an environment-variable based backend that supports a naming convention with optional defaults and best-effort type coercion. This subsystem matters because other octoDNS components rely on it to obtain configuration-time values, and errors or coercion differences can directly affect provider/plan behavior.
key_behaviors: Provides a common secret integration base that standardizes per-instance logging and naming context for secret backends.
Resolves secret values by name from the host process environment using a fetch(name, source) interface.
Interprets a special delimiter convention in the requested secret name to support fallback defaults when the env var is missing.
Performs lightweight type coercion on retrieved (or default) values, converting to int or float when heuristically applicable.
Raises a secret-specific exception type when resolution fails and no default is available, enabling callers to distinguish secret errors.

insights.title: Default fallback is encoded in the secret name string
insights.problem: Callers may request secrets using a single string that embeds both the environment variable name and an optional default value.
insights.root_cause: The backend parses requested secret names containing '/' into (env_var_name, default_value).
insights.solution: When modifying or consuming this backend, preserve the exact parsing and fallback behavior so missing env vars reliably fall back only when a default is specified.

insights.title: Value type depends on heuristics that may differ across call paths
insights.problem: Environment secret values can be interpreted as int, float, or string depending on content, and regressions can occur if coercion rules change or are inconsistently applied elsewhere.
insights.root_cause: The backend attempts conversion based on string content (notably whether '.' is present), which can lead to inconsistent outcomes across different construction/usage paths.
insights.solution: Maintain the coercion heuristics and ensure any changes are consistent with how secret values are expected to be used by manager/provider config assembly.

insights.title: Secret resolution failures are represented by a dedicated exception type
insights.problem: Secret resolution failures must be distinguishable from unrelated runtime errors so higher-level workflows can react appropriately.
insights.root_cause: The subsystem defines and raises a dedicated SecretsException base class (via EnvironSecretsException for env backend failures).
insights.solution: Keep exception types and raising conditions stable so callers relying on exception categorization (tests and control flow) continue to work.

insights.title: Logs are scoped to backend class and secret name
insights.problem: Secret resolution issues need consistent attribution to the relevant backend and secret key.
insights.root_cause: BaseSecrets initializes a logger keyed by class name and secret name.
insights.solution: Preserve the logger construction inputs and ensure any refactors keep the same logging scope so incident triage remains consistent.

key_files.why: Implements the core environment-backed secret resolution logic, including parsing defaults and type coercion.
Defines the BaseSecrets class used by secret backends for standardized logging and naming context.
Defines the common SecretsException type that callers and tests use to identify secret subsystem failures.
Establishes the package boundary; while it contains no logic, import structure may affect how backends are referenced elsewhere.

construct_relationships: EnvironSecrets (in environ.py) is the concrete secret backend that relies on the BaseSecrets logging/naming conventions and raises exception types derived from SecretsException (via EnvironSecretsException).
Callers elsewhere in the project depend on EnvironSecrets.fetch(name, source) to return resolved secret values (string/int/float) or raise a secret-specific exception when resolution fails.
notable_relationships: Upstream dependency: provider/config/manager construction flows likely call into this secret backend to obtain runtime configuration values before processing plan/changes; incorrect coercion or exception behavior can cascade into provider planning/sync outcomes.
Downstream dependency: error handling and tests elsewhere likely catch SecretsException (or backend-specific derivatives) to provide user-facing messages or to abort workflows safely.
recommendations: When editing environ.py, preserve the name parsing convention using '/' (env_var_name/DEFAULT_VALUE) and the exact fallback behavior when the environment variable is missing.
Maintain the existing type coercion expectations for env/default values (int vs float vs string) to avoid changing the semantic type of configuration inputs consumed by other modules.
Preserve exception class hierarchy and raising conditions (SecretsException and derived types) so callers that distinguish secret errors continue to function.
Keep BaseSecrets logger scoping behavior consistent (how logger name is derived from class and secret name) to avoid breaking log correlation or tests that assert log output.

octodns/source.description: The octodns/source package defines the contract and implementations for loading “current” DNS state into octoDNS zones. It matters because all downstream planning/sync logic relies on sources to consistently populate records with correct types, TTL behavior, and capability signaling (e.g., dynamic/geo support). This subtree includes an abstract base interface plus concrete sources for environment-variable TXT injection and legacy TinyDNS import, both of which must conform to the shared populate/support rules.
key_behaviors: Provide a standardized populate workflow for turning backend data (or environment values) into octoDNS zone record sets
Advertise and enforce source capabilities via required subclass attributes and support checks (including dynamic/geo support flags)
Ensure record-type compatibility by validating supported record types before populating zones
Implement deterministic record generation for environment variable sources (single TXT record derived at runtime)
Parse legacy TinyDNS files by interpreting symbols, aggregating multiple lines into multi-value records, and generating reverse PTR records when applicable

insights.title: BaseSource enforces subclass capabilities and populate expectations
insights.problem: Inconsistent or incomplete source implementations can break planning assumptions or cause runtime errors when downstream code expects specific attributes/behaviors.
insights.root_cause: BaseSource defines required capability attributes and shared logic, and relies on correct subclass definitions for supports/validation behaviors.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve BaseSource’s expected interface shape (class attributes and method semantics) so all source implementations continue to be discoverable and compatible with manager/provider orchestration.

insights.title: Dynamic/geo support flags must match actual behavior
insights.problem: Downstream systems may treat sources as dynamically varying (or geo-aware) when they are not, leading to incorrect change detection or planning results.
insights.root_cause: EnvVarSource explicitly disables geo/dynamic support because it always produces a deterministic TXT record derived from a current environment variable at execution time.
insights.solution: Any changes to how sources read external inputs or generate records must keep their SUPPORTS_DYNAMIC/geo-related signaling aligned with real determinism and variability.

insights.title: TinyDNS import must preserve mapping correctness and multi-value aggregation
insights.problem: Incorrect symbol-to-record-type mapping or loss of multi-line aggregation can produce incorrect record sets and therefore incorrect sync plans.
insights.root_cause: TinyDnsBaseSource uses a SYMBOL_MAP and parsing logic to translate TinyDNS symbols into octoDNS record types, including special handling for reverse/ARPA and generic ':' rdata parsing via registered types.
insights.solution: Changes affecting parsing logic must ensure symbol mapping coverage, aggregation behavior, and reverse-zone PTR generation semantics remain consistent with existing expectations and record registration mechanisms.

insights.title: EnvVarSource must handle missing/malformed environment variables predictably
insights.problem: If environment variable retrieval errors are not surfaced consistently, orchestration may fail non-deterministically or produce empty/incorrect zones.
insights.root_cause: EnvVarSource defines EnvironmentVariableNotFoundException and reads a specific env var during populate.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve exception behavior and how missing variables are reported, since upstream callers may rely on specific failure modes.

insights.title: TinyDNS default TTL when unspecified impacts change detection
insights.problem: If TTL derivation changes, the plan may show widespread diffs even when record content is unchanged.
insights.root_cause: TinyDnsBaseSource applies a default TTL when TinyDNS lines do not specify one.
insights.solution: Any modifications around TTL parsing or defaulting must keep resulting TTL values stable and predictable for existing inputs.

key_files.why: Defines BaseSource, the core interface and shared validation/support logic all source implementations must conform to.
Implements EnvVarSource that injects environment-derived TXT records and enforces deterministic-capability semantics.
Implements TinyDnsFileSource/TinyDnsBaseSource for parsing TinyDNS files, mapping symbols to record types, and generating reverse PTR records.
Package initializer that establishes the octodns.source namespace; may affect imports/discovery patterns for source classes.

construct_relationships: BaseSource defines the populate contract that EnvVarSource and TinyDnsFileSource implement to convert backend inputs into zone record structures
BaseSource.supports and record-type support checks gate whether specific record types may be populated by a source
EnvVarSource.populate reads environment variables (via an internal helper) and creates/overlays a TXT record in the zone
TinyDnsBaseSource._process_lines/_process_symbols interpret TinyDNS file lines/symbols, mapping them into octoDNS record types and leveraging Record.registered_types for generic ':' rdata parsing
TinyDnsBaseSource generates PTR records for reverse (ARPA) zones based on detected zone direction
notable_relationships: Downstream planning/sync logic depends on populated zones being internally consistent with octoDNS record type registrations and BaseSource’s declared support/capability flags

TinyDNS parsing relies on the global/central record-type registry (Record.registered_types) to interpret arbitrary rdata for ':' symbols, creating a coupling between source parsing and record definitions elsewhere in the project
EnvVarSource behavior and capability signaling couple runtime execution environment to deterministic zone generation, affecting how orchestration perceives source stability across runs
recommendations: When modifying BaseSource or its contract, ensure all subclasses in this package still satisfy required capability attributes and do not break support/validation expectations used by upstream orchestration
